text: Urology performs cystoscopy, revealing a fistula tract to the left of midline towards the bladder dome. A wire is used to cannulate the tract. Minimally invasive surgery follows with flexible sigmoidoscopy, closing the fistula using an over-the-scope clip. A panendoscopy of the bladder shows the fistula tract, and an angled guide wire is advanced through an open-ended catheter. Due to difficulty reaching the colon, a rigid bridge guides a curved catheter into the tract, allowing a hydrophilic guide wire to be fed through.

Under fluoroscopy, the catheter is further advanced and its placement within the colon is confirmed. The guide wire is then advanced into the colon. A flexible gastroscope is inserted into the colon, where the wire is identified, grasped, and extracted through the rectum.
The surgeon backloads a GIF-II-T scope with a XII-VI-T type over-the-scope clip and advances it over the guide wire to the level of the fistula.

The surgeon uses a combination of wire pull and colon manipulation to position the scope directly facing the defect. Aggressive suction is applied, drawing the wall of the colon into the cap. The wire is removed, and the clip is fired, visibly closing the endoscopic defect with the clip appearing in a good position.

Fluoroscopic evaluation shows contrast reaching the clip without passing it, confirming closure of the fistula. The surgeon ablates the fistula on the bladder side using a holmium laser, and a Foley catheter is placed to drain the bladder.
